metadata.title: Why a Quantum Packet Becomes Chirped as It Spreads
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = high-k front / leading edge / shorter wavelength; CRIMSON = low-k rear / trailing edge / longer wavelength
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: no complex-width Gaussian algebra, no quadratic-phase derivation, no radar/sonar technology tangent, no chirped-pulse amplification. Wonder register.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. med davy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

props.headline: Medhavy AI
props.body:
मेधावी (Medhavy): From Sanskrit, meaning
"intelligent" or "intellectually brilliant"
— the perfect name for our AI-powered
intelligent learning systems.

beats.narration_text: After a quantum packet travels for a while, its front edge oscillates faster than its back edge. The wavelength varies continuously from the rear to the front. The packet becomes chirped.

card.copy: Why a Quantum Packet Becomes Chirped as It Spreads

build.suggested: suggested prompt: After a quantum packet travels for a while, its front edge oscillates faster than its back edge. The wavelength varies continuously from the rear to the front.

beats.narration_text: We know packets spread: tight position means broad momentum range, and different momenta travel at different speeds. But spreading also means the components sort themselves in space. The fast components race ahead, the slow ones trail behind.

production_viz.label: Gaussian packet at t=0 and t>0, spreading shown

beats.narration_text: A packet starts with central momentum k-naught and spread delta-k. After time t, where are the high-k and low-k components? What does the packet look like locally — what is its local wavelength at the front versus the rear?

card.copy: After spreading: where are the high-k and low-k components? What is the local wavelength front vs. rear?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A packet starts with central momentum k-naught and spread delta-k. After time t, where are the high-k and low-k components? What does the packet look like local

beats.narration_text: Each Fourier component travels at its own group velocity — h-bar k over m. High-k components are faster. After time t, the component at k-naught plus delta-k has moved further than the component at k-naught minus delta-k. The packet is sorted: high-k at the front, low-k at the rear.

production_viz.label: high-k components at front (TEAL), low-k at rear (CRIMSON)

beats.narration_text: The local wavenumber at position x at time t is approximately k-naught plus x minus group-velocity times t, divided by h-bar t over m. In the leading half of the packet — ahead of center — the local k is greater than k-naught. In the trailing half, it's less. This gradient is chirp.

production_viz.label: k_local(x) gradient: high at front, low at rear, k0 at center

beats.narration_text: For an electron packet with k-naught equal to 5 per nanometer and delta-k equal to 1 per nanometer, after 100 femtoseconds: the front of the packet has local k about 5.8 per nanometer, the rear has about 4.2 per nanometer. A 16 percent variation in local momentum — visible as shorter wavelengths at the front.

production_viz.label: k_front=5.8/nm vs k_rear=4.2/nm after 100fs; wavelength ruler overlaid

beats.narration_text: This chirp is not noise or imperfection — it is the necessary consequence of spreading. The packet that started with uniform phase now carries a spatial frequency gradient. Amplitude is still Gaussian. But the local wavelength varies continuously from the trailing to the leading edge.

production_viz.label: Gaussian envelope unchanged; local frequency gradient = chirp

beats.narration_text: Radar uses chirped pulses deliberately. A frequency-swept microwave pulse — high frequency at start, low frequency at end — spreads in time as it propagates, exactly like a quantum packet. The receiver uses matched filtering to compress the echo back to a sharp spike. Same mathematics, different physics.

production_viz.label: chirped radar pulse: same math as quantum packet chirp

beats.narration_text: The chirped free packet contains the same information as the original, just rearranged in space. If you could apply a correcting phase — a negative chirp — you could re-compress the packet to its original width. This is the principle of chirped-pulse amplification in ultrafast lasers.

production_viz.label: negative chirp phase -> re-compress packet to original width

beats.narration_text: Free packet spreads. High-k at front, low-k at rear. Local wavelength varies continuously from back to front. This frequency gradient in space is chirp. Faster components left first, carrying their shorter wavelengths to the leading edge.

card.copy: Spreading → high-k front, low-k rear → local wavelength gradient front-to-back. That gradient is chirp.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Free packet spreads. High-k at front, low-k at rear. Local wavelength varies continuously from back to front. This frequency gradient in space is chirp. Faster

beats.narration_text: med davy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at med davy A-I. med davy dot com.
